title: Destiny 2 | End of Active Development, Bungie Layoffs & Full Legacy Hub
subtitle: After 12 years of continuous updates, Bungie has ended active development on Destiny 2. This hub covers the final update, the studio's collapse, Sony's write-down, and what remains.

table_of_contents.label: What the Final Patch Actually Changed
Destiny 2 Legacy | What It Leaves Behind
What Comes Next | Marathon and Bungie's Future

content_html:
Overview

Destiny 2 launched in September 2017 as Bungie's first independent release following the studio's separation from Microsoft and Activision. Over the next nine years it became one of the most ambitious live-service shooters ever built, cycling through dozens of expansions, seasonal content drops, and complete mechanical overhauls. At its peak, the game had tens of millions of registered accounts and a dedicated competitive scene.

On June 9, 2026, Bungie shipped the Monument of Triumph update, the final planned content patch for Destiny 2. No further expansions, seasonal content, or balance patches are scheduled. The game enters a static archive state, servers remaining online but development ending entirely. It is one of the largest planned live-service shutdowns in gaming history.

Monument of Triumph | The Final Update

Monument of Triumph, released June 9, 2026, is Bungie's send-off to a game they built for over a decade. Rather than a celebratory content drop, the patch is a quality-of-life restoration project, returning systems that were removed or degraded during Destiny 2's mid-life turbulence and adding one final endgame activity.

The update shipped alongside a free-to-play accessibility expansion, making the full content catalog available to all players with no paywall, a last-ditch effort to bring lapsed players back for the finale.

What the Final Patch Actually Changed

  • Director UI Restoration, the original destination map interface returned after years of being replaced by a more streamlined but less readable version
  • The Gauntlet, a new boss rush activity opening June 13, 2026, drawing from the full roster of Destiny 2's 9-year enemy library
  • All Exotic armor automatically upgraded to Tier 5, the game's highest stat tier, at no cost to players
  • Legacy raid gear set bonuses restored across all Year 1 through Year 6 raid sets
  • Warlock double Nova Bomb aspect re-enabled, a fan-favorite ability removed during a 2024 balance pass
  • The Collection, a curated museum mode showcasing every ship, ghost, and Exotic weapon from the game's full run, added to the Director

Bungie Layoffs | How the Studio Collapsed

The end of Destiny 2 development is inseparable from the unraveling of Bungie as an independent studio. After Sony acquired Bungie in 2022 for $3.6 billion, the studio faced a cascading series of internal crises.

In July 2023, Bungie cut approximately 100 employees in its first post-acquisition layoff. A larger cut followed in October 2024, removing roughly 220 employees, approximately 17 percent of the studio's total headcount at the time. A third round of cuts in early 2026 brought total post-acquisition layoffs to an estimated 400 positions.

The layoffs were attributed to multiple factors: disappointing sales of the The Final Shape expansion, the commercial underperformance of Marathon at launch, and Sony's broader restructuring of its first-party portfolio following a period of elevated spending across the gaming industry.

Sony's $700M Write-Down

Sony Interactive Entertainment has written down more than $700 million on its Bungie acquisition across its fiscal reporting since 2023. The write-downs reflect a reassessment of Bungie's projected future earnings relative to the $3.6 billion acquisition price, a gap that widened as both Destiny 2's player base declined and Marathon failed to establish itself as the live-service successor the deal was premised on.

Sony has not commented publicly on whether Bungie will remain an independent studio or be absorbed more directly into SIE's first-party structure following the end of Destiny 2 development.

Servers | Will Destiny 2 Stay Online?

Bungie has confirmed that Destiny 2 servers will remain online indefinitely following the end of active development. The game enters what the studio calls a static archive state: all existing content remains playable, matchmaking continues to function for activities with sufficient player populations, and no content will be vaulted or removed.

Bungie has not committed to a specific server sunset date, and no end-of-life announcement has been made. The static archive approach mirrors how older MMOs like City of Heroes (via private servers) and RuneScape Classic have been handled, though Destiny 2 will continue to be officially hosted rather than handed to the community.

Destiny 2 Legacy | What It Leaves Behind

Destiny 2's nine-year run produced some of the most mechanically sophisticated and artistically ambitious content in the live-service era. The Forsaken expansion (2018) is widely regarded as one of the best expansions in shooter history. The Shadowkeep, Beyond Light, and The Witch Queen campaigns demonstrated that live-service games could tell genuine narrative stories rather than just content delivery vehicles.

The game also leaves behind a cautionary arc. Its decision to vault entire expansions worth of content in 2020, known as the Content Vault controversy, permanently damaged player trust. The turbulent development of its final years, marked by layoffs, cancelled projects, and a communication breakdown between Bungie and its community, stands as one of the clearest case studies in how live-service games can exhaust both their developers and audiences simultaneously.

What Comes Next | Marathon and Bungie's Future

Bungie's next major release is Marathon, a squad-based extraction shooter set in the Marathon universe from the studio's pre-Halo history. Marathon launched in early 2026 to mixed commercial results, generating significant Day 1 interest but failing to retain a large concurrent player base past its first month.

Whether Marathon becomes the live-service anchor that justifies Sony's acquisition cost, or whether Bungie pivots to a different model entirely, is the central question facing the studio heading into the post-Destiny era. As of June 2026, no new projects have been officially announced.

Coverage by Jack Sterling, Senior Reporter at OzoneNews. See our full video games hub for ongoing Bungie and Marathon coverage.
